speaker: interviewer
text: This is tape number 3. We're interviewing Bob Geminder-- Geminder. And this is September 24, 1995, and we're in Palos Verdes, California.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. And during this uprising was, of course, a very major event, you know, in the history and what have you. But I don't want to dwell on the history too much. But basically what happened was the underground that was brewing for so many years in Warsaw decided to rise up and take their own city, Warsaw, before the Russians came in. And, of course the Russians, being as clever as they were at that point-- the war had already turned, because they were now marching through Poland towards-- towards Berlin. They stopped, and they let, basically, the Germans demolish the city of Warsaw. And they didn't take it, because even at that point, they already had their eyes on occupying Poland. And by-- by essentially demoralizing them and defeating the Polish underground, which was the only military part left in Poland, they could see that would be easier for them to occupy. But anyway, getting back to me-- we were then in-- in this uprising. We were caught in the middle of this uprising-- bombs, grenades, tanks-- you name it. I mean, we're right in the middle of a-- you know, the Polish underground versus the Germans. And the Germans were putting everything into it that they had to take Warsaw back. And we were in this tall building, where-- where Szutka lived, in this apartment. And we were-- we were there. In fact, one night, I remember sleeping-- we were still upstairs sleeping. And I was sleeping on a floor, like a mat on the floor. And there was this huge mirror that was just where my head was right here, just straight up like that. And I was sleeping, and a bomb fell-- not on our building, but, like, next-- very, very close. And this mirror shattered into a jillion pieces. And it was funny-- I was covered with glass from head to toe. Just everything fell on me. And yet there wasn't a scratch on me.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I've never been able to figure that out, how that was possible. But there was like a sheet of cover over me through some of this part of the war. And I guess that's why I'm here. So, at that point, when they started bombing these buildings, of course we all went down in the cellars, and we stayed in the cellars. The way it was done, the uprising, was that they had actually put holes in the cellar. So you could do all your walking or moving around underground. So, you know, all those buildings had cellars, and you could go from one building to another, because there was a big hole in there. And George and I had the distinct job-- I was sitting there-- because there were always people walking through, you know? They were moving. And there were so-- Was this done during the war, these holes?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, this all came about, like, in 1944.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: And these holes were put in there so that people could move underground without being on top of the ground, where they would be liable to get shot. And I still know my left from my right because of those incidents. This is now when I was nine. People would come through, and they would ask George-- they would ask me a question. George was there, too.

speaker: interviewer
text: They would ask me a question. Which way is such and such? And I would have to say 'the left hole,' which just meant this side. So we were sitting there all day, just kind of-- and this was, you know, kids-- how kids entertain themselves, I guess. People would come through. We'd say-- they would ask us a question-- the left hole to go through. Because the right one didn't go-- the left. So when I think about left and right, I still think about those situations, that situation.

speaker: interviewee
text: But anyway, we were there in that building, kind of-- this lasted, just to give you a time frame, about three months-- two, three months. I remember even one incident where the Americans flew over Warsaw. And, of course, the Americans knew what was going on. And they wanted to send supplies down. They wanted to help the underground.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the sky was just totally full with parachutes coming down and packages coming down. And one landed very close to the building we were in. And there was this big discussion in the cellar when I was there-- who's going to go get it? And somebody finally said, I'll go get it.

speaker: interviewee
text: And, well, he tried to get it, but he couldn't. He was shot trying to get it. So we never did get those American supplies during that attempt. But then the Germans started coming. They started taking street by street, building by building. And it was time for us to-- to escape. I remember the important thing I was told to do. I had this knapsack of tobacco. And my mother and Wujek said to me, you must keep this. This is something very important.

speaker: interviewer
text: Because they were looking ahead-- how are we going to live, possibly? You must keep this-- this tobacco bag. Tobacco was such a-- a big item. So I-- I was intent on keeping it. And when we were running across the street-- there was like a street which was-- we had thrown-- they had thrown all kinds of furniture on the street to make-- because they couldn't dig a trench. There wasn't enough time. But the furniture-- Barricade.

speaker: interviewee
text: --basically set up like a barricade for us. And we had to run across the street to keep running. So we ran across this street, and there were guns, bullets firing. In fact-- anyway, I dropped my bag of tobacco. I lost my bag of tobacco in all this excitement. In fact, Tadzik-- Tadzik is Szutka's husband. And again, to reiterate, Szutka was Wujek's sister. That's who fixed the papers, and that's where we escaped to. He got shot in the leg during this escape. But fortunately, the bullet went right through the meat part of the leg, so it didn't lodge itself. We were able to just bandage it up at the next place we could stop. And he could keep going.

speaker: interviewer
text: Anyway, basically, we-- we escaped and escaped until there was no place to escape anymore, and the Germans took the city. Now, at this point, you've got to realize, the Germans couldn't care less whether you were Jewish or non-Jewish. It didn't matter, OK? It was just-- everyone was-- was grouped together. Trains were brought in, and the trains were to concentration camps. And we-- Wujek was very, very smart. This is another one of those lifesaving episodes, you know? We had many, many where he saved our lives.

speaker: interviewee
text: But he said, stand back, stand back. Don't rush to get on the trains. Wait-- wait. So we waited and waited. And he finally found a car, a railcar, that was open on top. He said, that's the one we're going to go to. There were very few open cars. Most of them were the closed cars. And they pushed and shoved people in to these closed cars. And probably at their arrival point, my guess would be that a good percentage of these people were dead just from suffocation. But we made sure we got in an open car. So we got in this open-- and we were still able to somehow stay together. So there was the four of us, and Zutka and Tadzik were with us, so like the six of us there. And the train, of course-- we knew where it was going-- concentration camps. A lot of the Polish people didn't, because they were told they were going to work camps to work. We weren't told that. We knew that-- we knew better. And we also knew we were Jewish, so we had something else to worry about.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there very many Jewish people in your group at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: We have-- I have-- we have no idea.

speaker: interviewer
text: No way of knowing.

speaker: interviewee
text: No idea, no way of knowing at this point. I mean, everybody was afraid of everybody. You wouldn't talk to anyone. You wouldn't-- certainly about things like that. My educated guess would be no, because, at this point, the Jews were already-- most of them were gone. And, you know, this was 1944, I'm saying.

speaker: interviewer
text: So, getting back, we were on the train. The train stops for a red light, like, maybe a couple hundred yards before the gates. And Wujek takes this opportunity and jumps over the car, opens the door from the outside, and says, jump jump!

speaker: interviewee
text: And I remember-- oh my God, it was so high. I looked down, and it was like-- you know, at least to a little kid, it was like-- it looked like yards and yards away that I had to jump. But I jumped. And we jumped. In fact, I had-- in Europe, you wore long socks in those days. And I had like a rubber band around my socks to hold them up. And when I jumped, this rubber band really dug into my foot. And I remember how terrible that hurt. It just hurt something awful. But we jumped and started running towards-- towards a farmhouse. And-- and it's really strange. Most of the people didn't follow. We knew-- here's a case for being Jewish. We survived because we knew. The other people didn't have the kind of fear we had, you know, so it was-- Some of them, later-- we heard shots, or I heard shots. So some of them must have started jumping and running from the train. But unfortunately, at this point, it was too late.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was this daytime, nighttime?

speaker: interviewee
text: Night-- it was-- it was nighttime-- definitely dark. But, you know, the flashlights and the lights were on these people. And most of them, at least from what we heard, did not survive.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we went to-- to hide with a-- a family. And actually, you were kind of heroes in those days, because we were from the uprising, from the Polish Warsaw uprising. So the farm people would take us in-- again, no knowledge of Jewish or not Jewish. This was just people.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they took us in, and they hid us in these farmhouses until kind of that search. Because the Germans started searching. The Germans were very exact. They started searching homes and searching farmhouses. But basically, they hid us in these-- in this home-- in these farmhouses.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we stayed there. And we actually even moved from this, closer towards Warsaw again, towards a little town close to Warsaw. Because, again, we were trying to get away from the Germans and go to an area where the Russians might come. The Germans destroyed Warsaw during this uprising. I mean, Warsaw was pretty well intact, because there was no resistance in 1941 when they took Warsaw. But now with this uprising, Warsaw was essentially, like, 60%, 70% destroyed, through this uprising.

speaker: interviewee
text: Anyway, the Russians took the city. And at this point, we were somewhat relaxed, but we were still living as Gentiles. As I recall, we went back to making cigarettes. What works before worked then. And the Russians finally came in. And the Russians came in so fast to our village that they would pass up villages. And actually, you didn't even know it, but the Germans didn't even know it. This was, like, a little bit of chaos at this point-- not very well-- not good communication. I mean, the Germans would already have covered this city, and the Germans didn't even know it. In our particular city, little city we were in, the Germans were still riding around in carriages. They were still making plans to kill all the people. And the Russians had already taken the city, and they didn't even know it.

speaker: interviewer
text: So finally the second wave of Russians took our city. And I remember standing on the street, waving and cheering. And-- and I did know the alphabet, even though I had not gone to school, because there was some learning at home from my mother and Wujek. And I remember every one of these tanks, the Russian tanks, had on it 'Made in USA,' 'Made in USA'-- every one of them. It was all equipment that came from USA.

speaker: interviewee
text: And, you know, when the Russians came, there was no real problem. The Russians were-- they were looters. They would come into houses and pick up big alarm clocks and put them on their wrists, you know. But they were-- a lot of them were, you know, not well-educated. And-- but they basically-- first of all, they didn't give a darn whether you were Jewish or non-Jewish, so, just people. They were just after things, in many cases. So it wasn't bad. Once they took us, life was getting better, you know, we had-- you were able to get food. You were able to do things. You were able-- there was a certain amount of freedom. And basically the war was over for us at that time. And we even moved-- at this point, we moved out of this little city and went back to Bielsko. Bielsko is where my mother grew up. Incidentally, it's the city where my brother was born, also, in Bielsko. I was from Wroclaw. But we went back to Bielsko and went to our apartments, the apartment building we had there, and lived there, got an apartment and lived there. This was probably already after the war was over-- literally over, not just for us. In other words, the Russians had already taken Berlin, and the war was over. So we're now talking '45-- mid-'45. And so we started-- Wujek started trading, making some money, and things happened that way.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were rents being paid on the apartments, or was anything being managed?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, it was, of course, taken by the Nazis. And they were being paid, but certainly not to us-- to the city administration and what have you. We paid rent when we moved-- when we were there. In fact, kind of the incident, you might say, that got me to the United States was-- George and I went to a movie, our first movie. This is now 1945. I'm 10 years old, and he's 12.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the name of the movie was [LAUGHS]-- a classic, right? And with Polish subtitles. And we went to the movie. We saw the movie. And when the movie was over-- of course, there were a lot-- this was like an afternoon movie, obviously. There were a lot of kids there.

speaker: interviewee
text: And when we left the movie, we were going back towards our place, and some kids started screaming at us-- 'Jews, Jews!' Now these are the Polish kids. 'Jews!' And they started throwing stones at us. And we came-- we ran home, of course, and told my mother this. And my mother said, OK, that's it. We're leaving. Because Wujek really wanted to stay in Poland. You know, his sister was there. He knew the language. He knew how to do business. She said no, we're leaving. So he wanted to go to Israel. She wanted to go to USA. Basically, she said, you want to go to Israel, you go to Israel, because I'm not going. We're finished struggling. We're going to America.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we left-- we left Poland and-- for the United States. And the way we did it, you know-- it was fairly early in the game. So the borders were closed, but they weren't as closed as they became later.

speaker: interviewee
text: We actually went from Poland to Czechoslovakia. And you could go to Czechoslovakia on a one-day pass. And we did that. And, of course, we never came back. And we had some family in-- in Czechoslovakia, so we stayed with-- with that family, one of these cousins I mentioned way back early. His name is Isko Geminder. And was in a-- he survived because he was in the Russian army. He was an officer in the Russian army. His wife somehow survived Auschwitz. But she did. And they were already in a city called Morawska-Ostrawa in Czechoslovakia. So my mother took us there, and we stayed there while she went back and got some things. And then they came over to Czechoslovakia the same way. And we stayed with them a while. And then we made plans to go to Germany. See, we had to go to West Germany, of course. Wasn't any good to go to East Germany. So, to West Germany-- that's why we went through Czechoslovakia. And we were able to-- to find a guide. And he took us from Czechoslovakia to West Germany. In fact, I remember the snow was so high that at one point we had to actually dig tunnels in the snow to go through-- through the border. But we managed it and got to West Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you get into any relocation centers at all? And also, did you have contact-- you said you had-- some of your father's brothers or family came to the United States.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, this was early in the game. We hadn't, of course, contacted them yet. But when we got to Germany, we went to a-- we somehow wound up in a little city called Regensburg. And there, there was an UNRRA camp, United Nations rehabilitation-type camp, which was set up-- I say 'United Nations,' but in reality, it was primarily US-run camp, for displaced people, for orphans, for-- primarily for children that had lost their parents. And again, this wasn't Jewish. This was-- the UNRRA camps didn't care. You could have been any-- any nationality.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we-- we went to-- we got to Germany, and we went to one of these camps, hoping that we-- they would take us into these camps. Well, we were at this point a family unit. You know, there was Wujek, my mother, George, and I. They said, well, you really don't fit this type of environment.

speaker: interviewee
text: And so we went to talk to, uh-- Rachel Greene was her name. She was a major, and she was the head of this camp, a lady from Chicago. And while we were there, a sergeant comes in by name of Shamus-- Shamus. In fact, he was-- he was at our wedding, in Pittsburgh later. But anyway, he comes in, and he hears our name. He says, you know my best friend, best friend through high school and school, was a guy by the name of Dave Geminder. Dave 'je-mine-der.' And, sure enough, he was a son of one of my father's brothers, who came to America. So, make a long story short-- shorter-- because of that connection and because we started talking, we somehow got into this camp. She said to my mother, you can come, but you'll have to work, take care of children, and so on. And so we got into this camp. And, of course, this was wonderful. This is where I learned to chew gum, and I learned to-- all the-- all the four-letter words in English. And I had lots of food. And everything was just fine at this camp. I mean, this was just, you know, the-- the highlight, considering what we'd been through. And we waited our turn. Basically, we waited our turn to come to the United States. Now, to answer your question, at this point, this is when there was contact made with people in the US. You know, there were many organizations after the war to find surviving Jews, and names were published in the paper, you know, like our name was published in various papers in the United States as survivors that were looking for family, you know. It turns out that Judy's aunt, my present wife's aunt, saw this and saw we were looking for some people she knew. And she showed it to them, that there were some people in Europe looking for them. So we were able to make contact and get some papers. Because even then, you needed somebody to sponsor you to come to the States. So in January 1947, we were actually finally allowed to come to the States.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now I lost track of one item. What happened to your grandmother, who was travelling with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, not my grandmother-- my grandmother never left the ghetto, the Stanislawow ghetto. She was killed in the Stanislawow ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. That was when I lost track.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, my mother had made arrangements for her to leave the ghetto. But unfortunately, like, two days later, they killed pretty much every-- two days or three days after we left the Stanislawow ghetto, all the Jews were killed, strung up on telephone wires. They were killed, anyway. So my grandmother, in fact-- my mother to this day is having a hard time with that, that she didn't take her mother with us. But she listened to the rabbi.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we came to the-- to America in January 1947. We landed in New York. And to greet us at the boat was Lola and Miriam. You remember Lola and Miriam, the lady that-- that took George and me under their skirts out of the Stanislawow ghetto? Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: They had survived the war, and they were already in New York. And my Aunt Sarah, which was my father's sister, who was living in New Jersey at this time-- Sarah Gerson-- her husband was-- was still alive, but-- I mean, her husband was alive. They both met us. In fact, when we landed in New York, we went to their house first for about a week.

speaker: interviewer
text: So, you know, just to go back for one second more-- like, of my immediate family-- because you asked about my grandmother-- just to straighten it out. My immediate family who perished in the war, of course, was my father, Menachim Mendel; my grandfather Ira, or 'Eye-ra' in this country; and Golde Glotzer; my uncle, my mother's brother, Artur Glotzer; and his wife and son. Wujek's family was pretty much totally wiped out. And, of course, there were many, many, many, many cousins. Who survived the war? I'd like to go into that a little bit. I mentioned Isko Geminder. He's the one in New Jersey that survived. Another cousin of mine by the name of Joe Rotersman, and he's a first cousin-- his mother was my father's sister. He also was one of these-- he was taken to the Russians. He was older than me, so the Russians took him. And he was in the army, and he survived the war. In fact, he lives here in Calabasas.

speaker: interviewee
text: In fact, an interesting story about him-- he kept following us all the way after the war, and he finally wound up in this UNRRA camp. And the only way he could get to America was-- Rachel Greene and he got married. So they were married, and they lived in Chicago for many, many years-- just a little different twist. Besides that, of course, Lola and Miriam survived the war. And their last name is Indik. Miriam got married here, so her name is now Miriam Tauber. I had two other half uncles that survived the war. My grandfather got married twice. My father was in the first batch, and then when he got married again, because his wife died, he had two more children. And both of those children survived the war. One lives in Israel. His name is Moishe Geminder. And one lives in Poland, and his name is Joseph. So that's basically who survived the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right, at this point you're still about 10 years old.

speaker: interviewer
text: 12, at this point. What starts happening at age 12? Does your mother get you starting into school?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, right.

speaker: interviewer
text: How was your English? Did you just learn the language? Well,

speaker: interviewee
text: We were very fortunate, I guess, when we got-- we left New Jersey and went to Pittsburgh, because Pittsburgh is where my mother had some family. Now, this is family on her side, not my father's side. And in Pittsburgh, we got into the school by merely looking at the age we were, and that's it. In other words, I was 12. 12, you went into the last of the sixth grade. So we got here in January. I started school in March of 1947. And the principal said, look, we're going to go by age. And so he kind of threw me into this last of the sixth grade. And I just kind of floundered around. I learned the language. I mean, they passed me, but there was no pass. It was just-- you know, I was just there to learn the language. And then by September, you know, after the summer, I started the seventh grade. But by this time, I already knew English quite well. And my brother started the ninth grade.

speaker: interviewer
text: So all you ever learned-- all you ever needed to know, you learned in kindergarten. For me, it was not true, because I didn't have any of the first six grades. And it wasn't important, you know? I missed out on a lot of good stories and whatever you learn in the first six grades.

speaker: interviewee
text: But I started basically in the seventh grade and went through high school. So I graduated when I was 18, basically what-- like every other child in America and-- high school. And then I went to college in Pittsburgh-- at that time, the school was called Carnegie Tech; now it's called Carnegie Mellon University-- and took up Engineering as-- as a curriculum. So I graduated when I was around 22 from engineering school in Pittsburgh.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did Jerzyk do at this time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Excuse me?

speaker: interviewer
text: What did Jerzyk do at this time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Jerzyk?

speaker: interviewer
text: Jerzyk.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, George? Well, he also was in school and went to college. He went to different-- different colleges. And he actually became a chemical engineer, and I became an electrical engineer. We-- I left Pittsburgh after I graduated college, went to Texas for a job. And then I went in the army in the Signal Corps, US Signal Corps. George stayed in Pittsburgh for a while. And then we moved-- moved to California. I moved to California after the Army. It turns out my brother moved also to California, and my parents came out. And we've been here ever since.

speaker: interviewer
text: So your family is here at that point--

speaker: interviewee
text: Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: --in time.
